subject: Chemistry
question_text: The number of electrons, protons, and neutrons in a species are equal to 18, 16 and 16 respectively. Assign the proper symbol to the species.
question_html: The number of electrons, protons, and neutrons in a species are equal to 18, 16 and 16 respectively. Assign the proper symbol to the species.
solution_text: The atomic number is equal to number of protons = 16. The element is sulphur (S). Atomic mass number = number of protons + number of neutrons = 16 + 16 = 32 Species is not neutral as the number of protons is not equal to electrons. It is anion (negatively charged) with charge equal to excess electrons = 18 – 16 = 2 Symbol is {tex}{}_{16}^{32}{{\text{S}}^{2 - }}{/tex}
solution_html:
The atomic number is equal to number of protons = 16. The element is sulphur (S).
Atomic mass number = number of protons + number of neutrons = 16 + 16 = 32
Species is not neutral as the number of protons is not equal to electrons. It is anion (negatively charged) with charge equal to excess electrons = 18 – 16 = 2
Symbol is {tex}{}_{16}^{32}{{\text{S}}^{2 - }}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The Vividh Bharati Station of All India Radio, Delhi, Broadcasts on a frequency of 1368 kHz (kilohertz). Calculate the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation emitted by the transmitter. Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong to?
question_html: The Vividh Bharati Station of All India Radio, Delhi, Broadcasts on a frequency of 1368 kHz (kilohertz). Calculate the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation emitted by the transmitter. Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong to?
solution_text: Frequency = 1368 kHz = 1368 x 10 3 Hz. (given) We know that, Frequency, {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} , Where {tex}{\lambda}{/tex} = Wavelength. {tex}1368 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{Hz}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{1368 \times 10^{3}} \mathrm{Hz}{/tex} {tex}=219.3 \mathrm{m}{/tex} It belong to radio wave part of electromagnetic spectrum.
solution_html:
Frequency = 1368 kHz = 1368 x 103 Hz. (given)

We know that, Frequency, {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} , Where {tex}{\lambda}{/tex} = Wavelength.
{tex}1368 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{Hz}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{\lambda}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{1368 \times 10^{3}} \mathrm{Hz}{/tex}{tex}=219.3 \mathrm{m}{/tex}
It belong to radio wave part of electromagnetic spectrum.

question_text: The wavelength range of the visible spectrum extends from violet (400 nm) to red (750 nm). Express these wavelengths in frequencies (Hz). (1nm = 10 –9 m)
question_html: The wavelength range of the visible spectrum extends from violet (400 nm) to red (750 nm). Express these wavelengths in frequencies (Hz). (1nm = 10–9 m)
solution_text: Using equation 2.5, frequency of violet light {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{400 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 7.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz Frequency of red light {tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{750 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 4.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz The range of the visible spectrum is from 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 to 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz in terms of frequency units.
solution_html:

{tex}v=\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{400 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
= 7.50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz
Frequency of red light

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate (a) wavenumber and (b) frequency of yellow radiation having wavelength 5800 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} .
question_html: Calculate (a) wavenumber and (b) frequency of yellow radiation having wavelength 5800 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}.
solution_text: Calculation of wavenumber {tex}(\bar{v}){/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5800 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} = 5800 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 cm = 5800 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{5800 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 1.724 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m -1 = 1.724 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m -1 Calculation of the frequency (ν) {tex}\bar{v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{5800 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 5.172 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s -1
solution_html:
1. Calculation of wavenumber {tex}(\bar{v}){/tex}

    {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5800 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex} = 5800 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm
    = 5800 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m
    {tex}\bar{v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{5800 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
    = 1.724 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m-1
    = 1.724 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m-1

  2. Calculation of the frequency (ν)

    {tex}\bar{v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{5800 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 5.172 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 s-1

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the energy of one mole of photons of radiation whose frequency is 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz.
question_html: Calculate the energy of one mole of photons of radiation whose frequency is 5 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 Hz.
solution_text: Energy (E) of one photon is given by the expression E = hν h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –34 J s ν = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 (given) E = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –34 J s) {tex}\times{/tex} (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 ) = 3.313 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J The energy of one mole of photons = (3.313 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J) {tex}\times{/tex} (6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 mol –1 ) = 199.51 kJ mol –1
solution_html:
Energy (E) of one photon is given by the expression
E = hν
h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10–34 J s
ν = 5{tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1 (given)
E = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10–34 J s) {tex}\times{/tex} (5 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1)
= 3.313 {tex}\times{/tex}10–19 J
The energy of one mole of photons
= (3.313 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 J) {tex}\times{/tex} (6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 mol–1)
= 199.51 kJ mol–1

question_text: A 100-watt bulb emits a monochromatic light of wavelength 400 nm. Calculate the number of photons emitted per second by the bulb.
question_html: A 100-watt bulb emits a monochromatic light of wavelength 400 nm. Calculate the number of photons emitted per second by the bulb.
solution_text: Power of the bulb = 100 watt = 100 J s –1 Energy of one photon E = hν = hc/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{400 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 4.969 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Number of photons emitted {tex}\frac{100 \mathrm{Js}^{-1}}{4.969 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} = 2.012 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 20 s -1
solution_html:
Power of the bulb = 100 watt
= 100 J s–1
Energy of one photon E = hν = hc/{tex}\lambda{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{400 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
= 4.969 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Number of photons emitted

subject: Chemistry
question_text: When electromagnetic radiation of wavelength 300 nm falls on the surface of sodium, electrons are emitted with a kinetic energy of 1.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J mol –1 . What is the minimum energy needed to remove an electron from sodium? What is the maximum wavelength that will cause a photoelectron to be emitted?
question_html: When electromagnetic radiation of wavelength 300 nm falls on the surface of sodium, electrons are emitted with a kinetic energy of 1.68 {tex}\times{/tex}105 J mol–1. What is the minimum energy needed to remove an electron from sodium? What is the maximum wavelength that will cause a photoelectron to be emitted?
solution_text: The energy (E) of a 300 nm photon is given by hn = hc/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3.0 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{300 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J The energy of one mole of photons = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 mol –1 = 3.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J mol –1 The minimum energy needed to remove one mole of electrons from sodium = (3.99 –1.68) 10 5 J mol –1 = 2.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J mol –1 The minimum energy for one electron = {tex}\frac{2.31 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text { electrons mol }^{-1}}{/tex} = 3.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J This corresponds to the wavelength {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h c}{E}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3.0 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{3.84 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} = 517 nm (This corresponds to green light)
solution_html:
The energy (E) of a 300 nm photon is given by
hn = hc/{tex}\lambda{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3.0 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{300 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
= 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
The energy of one mole of photons
= 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10–19 J {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex}1023 mol–1
= 3.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J mol–1
The minimum energy needed to remove one mole of electrons from sodium
= (3.99 –1.68) 105 J mol–1
= 2.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 J mol–1
The minimum energy for one electron
= {tex}\frac{2.31 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text { electrons mol }^{-1}}{/tex}
= 3.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
This corresponds to the wavelength
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h c}{E}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{s} \times 3.0 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}{3.84 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{/tex}
= 517 nm
(This corresponds to green light)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The threshold frequency ν 0 for metal is 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 . Calculate the kinetic energy of an electron emitted when radiation of frequency ν =1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 s –1 hits the metal.
question_html: The threshold frequency ν0 for metal is 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1. Calculate the kinetic energy of an electron emitted when radiation of frequency ν =1.0 {tex}\times{/tex}1015 s–1 hits the metal.
solution_text: According to Einstein’s equation Kinetic energy = ½ m e v 2 = h(ν – ν 0 ) = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –34 J s) (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 s –1 – 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 ) = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –34 J s) (10.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 – 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 ) = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –34 J s) {tex}\times{/tex} (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s –1 ) = 1.988 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J
solution_html:
According to Einstein’s equation Kinetic energy = ½ mev2 = h(ν – ν0)
= (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10–34 J s) (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 s–1 – 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1)
= (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–34 J s) (10.0 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1 – 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1)
= (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10–34 J s) {tex}\times{/tex} (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 s–1)
= 1.988 {tex}\times{/tex}10–19 J

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What are the frequency and wavelength of a photon emitted during a transition from n = 5 state to the n = 2 state in the hydrogen atom?
question_html: What are the frequency and wavelength of a photon emitted during a transition from n = 5 state to the n = 2 state in the hydrogen atom?
solution_text: Since n i = 5 and n f = 2, this transition gives rise to a spectral line in the visible region of the Balmer series. {tex}\Delta E=2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\left[\frac{1}{5^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex} = -4.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J It is an emission energy The frequency of the photon (taking energy in terms of magnitude) is given by {tex}v=\frac{\Delta E}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4.58 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex} = 6.91 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.0 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{6.91 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{Hz}}{/tex} = 434 nm
solution_html:
Since ni = 5 and nf = 2, this transition gives rise to a spectral line in the visible region of the Balmer series.
{tex}\Delta E=2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}\left[\frac{1}{5^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]{/tex}
= -4.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
It is an emission energy
The frequency of the photon (taking energy in terms of magnitude) is given by
{tex}v=\frac{\Delta E}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4.58 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex}
= 6.91{tex}\times{/tex}1014 Hz
{tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.0 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{6.91 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{Hz}}{/tex}= 434 nm

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The mass of an electron is 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg. If its kinetic energy is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 J, calculate its wavelength.
question_html: The mass of an electron is 9.1{tex}\times{/tex}10-31 kg. If its kinetic energy is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25 J, calculate its wavelength.
solution_text: Step I. Calculation of the velocity of an electron Kinetic energy = 1/2 mv 2 = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 J = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 kg m 2 s -2 {tex}\mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{2 \times\left(3.0 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right)}{/tex} = 65.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m 2 s -1 v = (65.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m 2 s -2 ) 1/2 = 8.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 ms -1 Step II. Calculation of wavelength of the electron According to de Broglie's equation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right) \times\left(8.12 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 0.08967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 m = 8967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m = 8967 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} 1 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} = 10 -10 m)
solution_html:
Step I.
Calculation of the velocity of an electron
Kinetic energy = 1/2 mv2 = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex}10-25 J = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25 kg m2s-2
{tex}\mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{2 \times\left(3.0 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right)}{/tex}= 65.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m2s-1
v = (65.9 {tex}\times{/tex}104 m2s-2)1/2 = 8.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 ms-1

Step II.
Calculation of wavelength of the electron
According to de Broglie's equation,
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right) \times\left(8.12 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex}
= 0.08967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 m = 8967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m = 8967 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} = 10-10 m)

question_html: A microscope using suitable photons is employed to locate an electron in an atom within a distance of 0.1 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}. What is the uncertainty involved in the measurement of its velocity?

question_text: A golf ball has a mass of 40g, and a speed of 45 m/s. If the speed can be measured within accuracy of 2%, calculate the uncertainty in the position.
question_html: A golf ball has a mass of 40g, and a speed of 45 m/s. If the speed can be measured within accuracy of 2%, calculate the uncertainty in the position.
solution_text: The uncertainty in the speed is 2%, i.e., 45 {tex}\frac{2}{100}{/tex} = 0.9 ms -1 Using the equation {tex}\Delta x=\frac{h}{4 \pi m \Delta v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 40 \mathrm{g} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\left(0.9 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 1.46 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –33 m This is nearly ~ 10 18 times smaller than the diameter of a typical atomic nucleus. As mentioned earlier for large particles, the uncertainty principle sets no meaningful limit to the precision of measurements.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the total number of orbitals associated with the principal quantum number n = 3?
question_html: What is the total number of orbitals associated with the principal quantum number n = 3?
solution_text: For n = 3, the possible values of l are 0, 1 and 2. Thus there is one 3s orbital (n = 3, l = 0 and m l = 0); there are three 3p orbitals (n = 3, l = 1 and m l = –1, 0, +1); there are five 3d orbitals (n = 3, l = 2 and m l = –2, –1, 0, +1+, +2). Therefore, the total number of orbitals is 1 + 3 + 5 = 9 The same value can also be obtained by using the relation; number of orbitals = n 2 , i.e. 3 2 = 9
solution_html:
For n = 3, the possible values of l are 0, 1 and 2.
Thus there is one 3s orbital (n = 3, l = 0 and ml = 0); there are three 3p orbitals (n = 3, l = 1 and ml = –1, 0, +1); there are five 3d orbitals (n = 3, l = 2 and ml = –2, –1, 0, +1+, +2).
Therefore, the total number of orbitals is 1 + 3 + 5 = 9
The same value can also be obtained by using the relation; number of orbitals = n2, i.e. 32 = 9

question_text: Calculate the total number of electrons present in one mole of methane. Find (a) the total number and (b) the total mass of neutrons in 7 mg of 14 C. (Assume that mass of a neutron = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –27 kg). Find (a) the total number and (b) the total mass of protons in 34 mg of NH 3 at STP. Will the answer change if the temperature and pressure are changed?
question_html:
1. Calculate the total number of electrons present in one mole of methane.
  2. Find (a) the total number and (b) the total mass of neutrons in 7 mg of 14C. (Assume that mass of a neutron = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–27 kg).
  3. Find (a) the total number and (b) the total mass of protons in 34 mg of NH3 at STP.
    Will the answer change if the temperature and pressure are changed?
solution_text: As we know in 1 molecule of methane, 1 carbon atom and 4 atoms of hydrogen is present. In carbon, there are six electrons and hydrogen consist of one electron each. So a total number of electrons in methane = 6 + 4 = 10 electrons By Avogadro’s Law, we know that 1 mole of methane contains 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 atoms. So the total number of electrons of in 1-mole methane = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 6.0 23 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 electrons So the total number of electrons in 1 mole of methane is 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 electrons Mass of one neutron = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –27 kg 1 mole of Carbon atom = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 atoms Number of neutrons in 1 carbon atom = 14 - 6= 8 So the total number of neutrons in 14g of Carbon = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 neutrons So 7mg of Carbon will contain = {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 8 \times 7 \times 10^{-3}}{14}{/tex} = [3.37288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 ] / 14 = 2.4092 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 21 neutrons So, Mass of 2.4092 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 21 neutrons = [2.4092 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 21 ] {tex}\times{/tex} [1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –24 ] = 4.035 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 g So, in 7 mg of carbon total number of neutrons is 2.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 21 and the total mass of the neutrons is 4.035 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 g Molecular Mass of Ammonia = 17g By Avogadro’s Law, 1 mole of Ammonia = 17g of Ammonia = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 23 atoms Total Number of Protons in Ammonia = 7 + 3= 10 So the total number of protons in Ammonia = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 protons 17g of Ammonia contains 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 protons So, 34 mg of Ammonia will contain X number of protons {tex}x=\frac{6.023 \times 10^{24} \times 34 \times 10^{-3}}{17}{/tex} X = [6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 ] {tex}\times{/tex} [2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ] X = 1.2046 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 protons Mass of one proton = 1.6726 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –24 g So, Mass of 1.2046 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 protons = [1.6726 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –24 ] {tex}\times{/tex} [1.2046 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 ] = 20.148 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 g So, in 34 mg of ammonia total number of protons is 1.205 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 and the total mass of the protons is 20.148 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 g. No, the answer will not vary with the change in temperature and pressure because the number of subatomic particles like protons, neutrons, and electrons is fixed for each and every element and it does not vary with temperature and pressure.
solution_html:
1. As we know in 1 molecule of methane, 1 carbon atom and 4 atoms of hydrogen is present.
    In carbon, there are six electrons and hydrogen consist of one electron each.
    So a total number of electrons in methane = 6 + 4 = 10 electrons
    By Avogadro’s Law, we know that
    1 mole of methane contains 6.023{tex}\times{/tex}1023 atoms. So the total number of electrons of in 1-mole methane = 10{tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 electrons
    So the total number of electrons in 1 mole of methane is 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 electrons
  2. Mass of one neutron = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–27 kg
    1 mole of Carbon atom = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms
    Number of neutrons in 1 carbon atom = 14 - 6= 8
    So the total number of neutrons in 14g of Carbon = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 neutrons
    So 7mg of Carbon will contain = {tex}\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 8 \times 7 \times 10^{-3}}{14}{/tex}
    = [3.37288{tex}\times{/tex}1022] / 14
    = 2.4092 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 neutrons
    So, Mass of 2.4092 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 neutrons = [2.4092 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021] {tex}\times{/tex} [1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–24]
    = 4.035 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 g
    So, in 7 mg of carbon total number of neutrons is 2.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 and the total mass of the neutrons is 4.035 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 g
  3. Molecular Mass of Ammonia = 17g
    By Avogadro’s Law,
    1 mole of Ammonia = 17g of Ammonia = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms
    Total Number of Protons in Ammonia = 7 + 3= 10
    So the total number of protons in Ammonia = 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 protons
    17g of Ammonia contains 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 protons
    So, 34 mg of Ammonia will contain X number of protons
    {tex}x=\frac{6.023 \times 10^{24} \times 34 \times 10^{-3}}{17}{/tex}
    X = [6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024] {tex}\times{/tex} [2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3]
    X = 1.2046 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 protons
    Mass of one proton = 1.6726 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–24g
    So, Mass of 1.2046 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 protons = [1.6726 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–24] {tex}\times{/tex} [1.2046 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022]
    = 20.148 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 g
    So, in 34 mg of ammonia total number of protons is 1.205{tex}\times{/tex}1022 and the total mass of the protons is 20.148{tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 g.

No, the answer will not vary with the change in temperature and pressure because the number of subatomic particles like protons, neutrons, and electrons is fixed for each and every element and it does not vary with temperature and pressure.

question_text: What is the number of photons of light with wavelength 4000 pm which provides 1 J of energy?
question_html: What is the number of photons of light with wavelength 4000 pm which provides 1 J of energy?
solution_text: Energy of photon (E) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js, c= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 , {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4000 pm = 4000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m {tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy of photon (E) = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(4 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 4.969 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J Now, 4.969 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J is the energy of photon = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 J is the energy of photons = {tex}\frac{1}{4.969 \times 10^{-17}}{/tex} = 2.012 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 16 photons.
solution_html:
Energy of photon (E) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex}
h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js, c= 3{tex}\times{/tex}108 ms-1, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4000 pm = 4000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-9 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Energy of photon (E) = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(4 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex}= 4.969 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J
Now, 4.969 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J is the energy of photon = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex}1 J is the energy of photons = {tex}\frac{1}{4.969 \times 10^{-17}}{/tex}= 2.012 {tex}\times{/tex}1016 photons.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: A photon of wavelength 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m strikes on the metal surface, the work function of metal being 2.13 eV. Calculate the energy of the photon (eV), the kinetic energy of emission, the velocity of the photoelectron. (1 eV = 1.6020 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J).
question_html:
A photon of wavelength 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m strikes on the metal surface, the work function of metal being 2.13 eV. Calculate

  1. the energy of the photon (eV),
  2. the kinetic energy of emission,
  3. the velocity of the photoelectron. (1 eV = 1.6020 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J).
solution_text: The energy of the photon Energy (E) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 4.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 = {tex}\frac{(1 \mathrm{eV})}{\left(1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)} \times{/tex} (4.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J) = 3.1 eV The kinetic energy of emission Kinetic energy of emission = E - work function (i.e. kinetic energy of emitted electron) = (3.1 - 2.13) = 0.97 eV Velocity of photoelectron KE of emission = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = 0.97 eV = 0.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 0.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 kg m 2 s -2 or v 2 = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.97 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\left(\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right)}{/tex} = 0.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 m 2 s -2 or v = (0.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 m 2 s -2 ) 1/2 = 0.583 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 = 5.83 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 ms -1
solution_html:
1. The energy of the photon
    Energy (E) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 4.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
    = {tex}\frac{(1 \mathrm{eV})}{\left(1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)} \times{/tex} (4.97 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J) = 3.1 eV
  2. The kinetic energy of emission
    Kinetic energy of emission = E - work function (i.e. kinetic energy of emitted electron)
    = (3.1 - 2.13) = 0.97 eV
  3. Velocity of photoelectron
    KE of emission =  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = 0.97 eV
    = 0.97 {tex}\times{/tex}1.602 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J = 0.97 {tex}\times{/tex}1.602 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 kg m2 s-2
    or v2 =  {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.97 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\left(\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right)}{/tex}= 0.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012 m2s-2
    or v = (0.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012 m2 s-2)1/2 = 0.583 {tex}\times{/tex}106 ms-1 = 5.83 {tex}\times{/tex}105 ms-1

{tex}\lambda{/tex}= 242 nm = 242 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex}108 ms-1, h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10-34 Js
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(242 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J
Ionisation energy per mol (E) = {tex}\frac{\left(0.0821 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{J}\right) \times\left(6.022 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{J}\right)}{1000}{/tex}
= 494 kJ mol-1

question_text: A 25 watt bulb emits monochromatic yellow light of wavelength 0.57 {tex}\mu \mathrm{m}{/tex} . Calculate the rate of emission of quanta per second.
question_html: A 25 watt bulb emits monochromatic yellow light of wavelength 0.57 {tex}\mu \mathrm{m}{/tex}. Calculate the rate of emission of quanta per second.
solution_text: The energy of one photon (E) = hv = hc/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js; C = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 ; {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.57 10 -6 m E = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(0.57 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Rate of emission of quanta per second = {tex}\frac{\text { Power }}{\text { Energy }}{/tex} Power (P) = 25 watt = 25Js -1 ; E = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = {tex}\frac{(25 \text { watt })}{\left(3.48 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)}=\frac{\left(25 \mathrm{Js}^{-1}\right)}{\left(3.48 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex} = 7.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 19 s -1
solution_html:
The energy of one photon (E) = hv = hc/{tex}\lambda{/tex} 
h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex}10-34 Js; C = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1; {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.57 10-6 m
E = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(0.57 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Rate of emission of quanta per second = {tex}\frac{\text { Power }}{\text { Energy }}{/tex}
Power (P) = 25 watt = 25Js-1; E = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
= {tex}\frac{(25 \text { watt })}{\left(3.48 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)}=\frac{\left(25 \mathrm{Js}^{-1}\right)}{\left(3.48 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex} = 7.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 1019 s-1

question_text: Electrons are emitted with zero velocity from a metal surface when it is exposed to radiation of wavelength 6800 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} . Calculate threshold frequency (V 0 )and work function (W 0 ) of the metal.
question_html: Electrons are emitted with zero velocity from a metal surface when it is exposed to radiation of wavelength 6800 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}. Calculate threshold frequency (V0)and work function (W0) of the metal.
solution_text: Threshold frequency (v 0 ) = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}\right)}{\left(68 \times 10^{-8} m\right)}=4.41 \times 10^{14} s^{-1}{/tex} Work function (W 0 ) = h v 0 = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js) {tex}\times{/tex} (4.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s -1 ) = 2.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J
solution_html:
Threshold frequency (v0) = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}\right)}{\left(68 \times 10^{-8} m\right)}=4.41 \times 10^{14} s^{-1}{/tex}
Work function (W0) = h v0 = (6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js) {tex}\times{/tex} (4.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 s-1) = 2.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the wavelength of light emitted when the electron in a hydrogen atom undergoes a transition from an energy level with n = 4 to an energy level with n = 2?
question_html: What is the wavelength of light emitted when the electron in a hydrogen atom undergoes a transition from an energy level with n = 4 to an energy level with n = 2?
solution_text: According to Rydberg's formula of spectral lines: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=109677\left(\frac{1}{n_1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_2^{2}}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} Here, n 1 =2 and n 2 =4. Put these values in above equation, we get. {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=109677\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 486 nm The colour corresponding to this wavelength is blue.
solution_html:
According to Rydberg's formula of spectral lines: {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=109677\left(\frac{1}{n_1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_2^{2}}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
Here, n1 =2 and n2 =4. Put these values in above equation, we get.
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}=109677\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right) \mathrm{cm}^{-1}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 486 nm
The colour corresponding to this wavelength is blue.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How much energy is required to ionise a H atom if the electron occupies n = 5 orbit? Compare your answer with the ionization enthalpy of H atom (energy required to remove the electron from n = 1 orbit).
question_html: How much energy is required to ionise a H atom if the electron occupies n = 5 orbit? Compare your answer with the ionization enthalpy of H atom (energy required to remove the electron from n = 1 orbit).
solution_text: Energy for a hydrogen electron present in a particular energy shell, {tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.12}{n^{2}} \times 10^{5} J \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{13.12 \times 10^{5}}{\mathrm{n}^{2} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}{/tex} Step I. Ionization energy for hydrogen electron present in orbit n = 5 {tex}{\rm{I}}{{\rm{E}}_5} = {{\rm{E}}_\infty } - {{\rm{E}}_5} = 0 - \left( {{{ - 2.18 \times {{10}^{ - 18}}} \over {25}}} \right){\rm{J atom}}{{\rm{ }}^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 8.72 \times {10^{ - 20}}{\rm{J}}{/tex} Step II. Ionization energy for hydrogen electron present in orbit n = 1. {tex}I E_{1}=E_{\infty}-E_{1}=0-\left(\frac{-2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{1}\right){/tex} = {tex}2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}{/tex} On comparing: {tex}\frac{I E_{5}}{\mathbb{I E}_{1}}=\frac{\left(8.72 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}\right)}{\left(2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 0.04 IE 1 is 25 times as compared to IE 5 .
solution_html:
Energy for a hydrogen electron present in a particular energy shell,
{tex}E_{n}=-\frac{13.12}{n^{2}} \times 10^{5} J \mathrm{mol}^{-1}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{13.12 \times 10^{5}}{\mathrm{n}^{2} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}{/tex}
Step I. Ionization energy for hydrogen electron present in orbit n = 5
{tex}{\rm{I}}{{\rm{E}}_5} = {{\rm{E}}_\infty } - {{\rm{E}}_5} = 0 - \left( {{{ - 2.18 \times {{10}^{ - 18}}} \over {25}}} \right){\rm{J atom}}{{\rm{ }}^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex} = 8.72 \times {10^{ - 20}}{\rm{J}}{/tex}
Step II. Ionization energy for hydrogen electron present in orbit n = 1.
{tex}I E_{1}=E_{\infty}-E_{1}=0-\left(\frac{-2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{1}\right){/tex}
= {tex}2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
On comparing: {tex}\frac{I E_{5}}{\mathbb{I E}_{1}}=\frac{\left(8.72 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}\right)}{\left(2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J} \text { atom }^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 0.04
IE1 is 25 times as compared to IE5.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the wave number for the longest wavelength transition in the Balmer atomic hydrogen.
question_html: Calculate the wave number for the longest wavelength transition in the Balmer atomic hydrogen.
solution_text: According to Balmer formula, {tex}\overline{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex} In order that the wavelength ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} )may be the maximum, wave number (v)must be the least. This is possible in case n 2 - n 1 is minimum. Now, for Balmer series, n 1 = 2 and n 2 must be 3. Substituting these values in the Balmer formula, {tex}\overline{\mathrm{v}}=\left(1.097 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=1.097 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{5}{36}\right)=1.523 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Balmer formula, {tex}\overline{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
In order that the wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex})may be the maximum, wave number (v)must be the least. This is possible in case n2 - n1 is minimum. Now, for Balmer series, n1= 2 and n2 must be 3. Substituting these values in the Balmer formula,
{tex}\overline{\mathrm{v}}=\left(1.097 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}\right){/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}=1.097 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{5}{36}\right)=1.523 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is the energy in joules, required to shift the electron of the hydrogen atom from the first Bohr orbit to the fifth Bohr orbit and what is the wavelength of the light emitted when the electron returns to the ground state? The ground-state electron energy is -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 ergs.
question_html: What is the energy in joules, required to shift the electron of the hydrogen atom from the first Bohr orbit to the fifth Bohr orbit and what is the wavelength of the light emitted when the electron returns to the ground state? The ground-state electron energy is -2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 ergs.
solution_text: Energy (E) associated with the nth Bohr orbit of an atom is: Where, Z denotes the atom's atomic number Ground state energy {tex}=-2.18 \times 10^{-11} \text {ergs }{/tex} {tex}=-2.18 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{-7} J{/tex} {tex}=-2.18 \times 10^{-18} J{/tex} The required energy for an electron shift from n = 1 to n = 5 is: {tex}\Delta E=E_{5}-E_{1}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\left(\frac{-\left(2.18 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{J}\right)(1)^{2}}{(5)^{2}}\right)-\left(-2.18 \times 10^{-18}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left(2.18 \times 10^{-18}\right)\left[1-\frac{1}{25}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left(2.18 \times 10^{-18}\right)\left[\frac{24}{25}\right]{/tex} {tex}=2.0928 \times 10^{-18} J{/tex} (1erg = 10 -7 J) The wavelength of the emitted light {tex}=\frac{h c}{E} \quad E=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}{\left(2.0928 \times 10^{-15}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=9.498 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
Energy (E) associated with the nth Bohr orbit of an atom is:
Where, Z denotes the atom's atomic number
Ground state energy {tex}=-2.18 \times 10^{-11} \text {ergs }{/tex}
{tex}=-2.18 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{-7} J{/tex}
{tex}=-2.18 \times 10^{-18} J{/tex}
The required energy for an electron shift from n = 1 to n = 5 is:
{tex}\Delta E=E_{5}-E_{1}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\left(\frac{-\left(2.18 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{J}\right)(1)^{2}}{(5)^{2}}\right)-\left(-2.18 \times 10^{-18}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left(2.18 \times 10^{-18}\right)\left[1-\frac{1}{25}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left(2.18 \times 10^{-18}\right)\left[\frac{24}{25}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=2.0928 \times 10^{-18} J{/tex} (1erg = 10-7J)
The wavelength of the emitted light {tex}=\frac{h c}{E} \quad E=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}{\left(2.0928 \times 10^{-15}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=9.498 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{m}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The electronic energy in hydrogen atom is given by E n = (-2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 )/n 2 J.Calculate the energy required to remove an electron completely from the n = 2 orbit. What is the longest wavelength of light in cm that can be used to cause this transition?
question_html: The electronic energy in hydrogen atom is given by En = (-2.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18)/n2J.Calculate the energy required to remove an electron completely from the n = 2 orbit. What is the longest wavelength of light in cm that can be used to cause this transition?
solution_text: Step I. Calculation of energy required The energy required is the difference in the energy when the electron jumps from orbit with n = {tex}\infty{/tex} to orbit with n = 2 The energy required ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} E) = E {tex}\infty{/tex} - E 2 = 0 - {tex}\left(-\frac{2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{4} \mathrm{J}\right){/tex} = 5.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Step II. Calculation of the longest wavelength of light in cm used to cause the transition {tex}\Delta{/tex} E = hv = hc/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\Delta \mathrm{E}}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(5.45 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex} = 3.644 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 3.644 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 = 3.645 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 cm
solution_html:
Step I. Calculation of energy required
The energy required is the difference in the energy when the electron jumps from orbit with n = {tex}\infty{/tex} to orbit with n = 2
The energy required ({tex}\Delta{/tex}E) = E{tex}\infty{/tex} - E2
= 0 - {tex}\left(-\frac{2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{4} \mathrm{J}\right){/tex} = 5.45 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
Step II. Calculation of the longest wavelength of light in cm used to cause the transition
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = hv = hc/{tex}\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\Delta \mathrm{E}}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(5.45 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}\right)}{/tex}
 = 3.644 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m = 3.644 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 = 3.645 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 cm

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the wavelength of an electron moving with a velocity of 2.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ms -1 .
question_html: Calculate the wavelength of an electron moving with a velocity of 2.05 {tex}\times{/tex}107 ms-1.
solution_text: According to de Broglie's equation {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex} Mass of electron (m) = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg Velocity of electron (v) = 2.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ms -1 Planck's constant (h) = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 km 2 s -1 {tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right) \times\left(2.05 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 3.55 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -11 m
solution_html:
According to de Broglie's equation {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex}
Mass of electron (m) = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex}10-31 kg
Velocity of electron (v) = 2.05 {tex}\times{/tex}107 ms-1
Planck's constant (h) = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 km2s-1
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right) \times\left(2.05 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex}
= 3.55 {tex}\times{/tex}10-11 m

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The mass of an electron is 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg. If its kinetic energy is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 J, calculate its wavelength.
question_html: The mass of an electron is 9.1{tex}\times{/tex}10-31 kg. If its kinetic energy is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25 J, calculate its wavelength.
solution_text: Step I. Calculation of the velocity of an electron Kinetic energy = 1/2 mv 2 = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 J = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 kg m 2 s -2 {tex}\mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{2 \times\left(3.0 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right)}{/tex} = 65.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m 2 s -1 v = (65.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 m 2 s -2 ) 1/2 = 8.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 ms -1 Step II. Calculation of wavelength of the electron According to de Broglie's equation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right) \times\left(8.12 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex} = 0.08967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 m = 8967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m = 8967 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} 1 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} = 10 -10 m)
solution_html:
Step I.
Calculation of the velocity of an electron
Kinetic energy = 1/2 mv2 = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex}10-25 J = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25 kg m2s-2
{tex}\mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{2 \times\left(3.0 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right)}{/tex}= 65.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m2s-1
v = (65.9 {tex}\times{/tex}104 m2s-2)1/2 = 8.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 ms-1

Step II.
Calculation of wavelength of the electron
According to de Broglie's equation,
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}\right) \times\left(8.12 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{/tex}
= 0.08967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 m = 8967 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m = 8967 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{A}{/tex} = 10-10 m)

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Show that the circumference of the Bohr orbit for the hydrogen atom is an integral multiple of the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving around the orbit?
question_html: Show that the circumference of the Bohr orbit for the hydrogen atom is an integral multiple of the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electron revolving around the orbit?
solution_text: According to Bohr's theory, {tex}\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3, . . . so on) or {tex}2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \mathrm{v}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{h}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} .... (i) According to de Broglie equation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{m} \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}{/tex} .... (ii) Comparing (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{h}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex} Thus, the circumference (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r) of the Bohr orbit for hydrogen atom is an into the de Broglie wavelength.
solution_html:
According to Bohr's theory,
 {tex}\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}{/tex}(n = 1, 2, 3, . . . so on)
or {tex}2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \mathrm{v}}{/tex}or {tex}\mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{h}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{/tex} .... (i)
According to de Broglie equation,
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}or {tex}\mathrm{m} \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}{/tex}.... (ii)
Comparing (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{n} \mathrm{h}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}{/tex}or {tex}2 \pi r=n \lambda{/tex}
Thus, the circumference (2{tex}\pi{/tex}r) of the Bohr orbit for hydrogen atom is an into the de Broglie wavelength.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer transition, n = 4 to n = 2 of He + spectrum?
question_html: What transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer transition, n = 4 to n = 2 of He+ spectrum?
solution_text: Applying Rydberg's Formula for the Balmer transition, n 2 = 4, to n 1 = 2 in a He + ion, We have, {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = Z ^ { 2 } R _ { H } \left( \frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}= Z ^ { 2 } R _ { H } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 4 ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 } { 4 } R _ { H}{/tex} ..........(i) Now, By applying Rydberg's Formula for a hydrogen atom, We have. {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R _ { H } \left( \frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} ........(ii) On equating equations (ii) and (i), we get. {tex}\frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} This equation gives n 1 = 1 and n 2 = 2. Thus the transition n = 2 to n = 1 in hydrogen ( 1 H)atom will have same wavelength as transition, n = 4 to n = 2 in He + .
solution_html:
Applying Rydberg's Formula for the Balmer transition, n2 = 4, to n1 = 2 in a He+ ion, We have,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = Z ^ { 2 } R _ { H } \left( \frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}{tex}= Z ^ { 2 } R _ { H } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 4 ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}{tex}= \frac { 3 } { 4 } R _ { H}{/tex}..........(i)

Now, By applying Rydberg's Formula for a hydrogen atom, We have.

{tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda } = R _ { H } \left( \frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}........(ii)

On equating equations (ii) and (i), we get.

{tex}\frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { n _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}

This equation gives n1 = 1 and n2 = 2. Thus the transition n = 2 to n = 1 in hydrogen (1H)atom will have same wavelength as transition, n = 4 to n = 2 in He+.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: In Rutherford’s experiment, generally the thin foil of heavy atoms, like gold, platinum etc. have been used to be bombarded by the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles. If the thin foil of light atoms like aluminium etc. is used, what difference would be observed from the above results?
question_html: In Rutherford’s experiment, generally the thin foil of heavy atoms, like gold, platinum etc. have been used to be bombarded by the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles. If the thin foil of light atoms like aluminium etc. is used, what difference would be observed from the above results?
solution_text: Heavy atoms such as gold, platinum have a heavy nucleus. The heavy nucleus contains a large amount of positive charge. When a beam of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles are shot at a thin gold foil most of them pass through without much effect. Some, however, are deflected back or by small angles due to the enormous repulsive force of heavy nucleus. If light aluminium foil is used, the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles deflected back or those deflected by small angles will be negligible. Therefore, In Rutherford's experiment, generally the thin foil of heavy atoms, like gold , platinum etc., have been used to be bombarded by the {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles.
solution_html:
Heavy atoms such as gold, platinum have a heavy nucleus. The heavy nucleus contains a large amount of positive charge. When a beam of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles are shot at a thin gold foil most of them pass through without much effect.
Some, however, are deflected back or by small angles due to the enormous repulsive force of heavy nucleus. If light aluminium foil is used, the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles deflected back or those deflected by small angles will be negligible. Therefore, In Rutherford's experiment, generally the thin foil of heavy atoms, like gold , platinum etc., have been used to be bombarded by the  {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles.

question_text: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7% more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
question_html: An element with mass number 81 contains 31.7% more neutrons as compared to protons. Assign the atomic symbol.
solution_text: We know that mass number of the element, A = p + n. Given, A= 81 Therefore, p +n = 81 .....(1) Let the number of protons, p =x Then, the number of neutrons, n = x+ {tex}\frac{31.7}{100} x=1.317 x{/tex} (As number of neutrons are 31.7% more than the protons.) Hence, from or Eq. (i) x+1.317 x = 81 or 2.317 x = 81 or {tex}x=\frac{81}{2.317}=34.958=35{/tex} . Since atomic number = Number of protons. Therefore, Atomic number of element = 35 (Bromine). The symbol of an element = {tex}{}_{35}^{81}Br{/tex} .
solution_html:

Given, A= 81

Therefore, p +n = 81 .....(1)
Let the number of protons, p =x
Then, the number of neutrons, n = x+ {tex}\frac{31.7}{100} x=1.317 x{/tex}  (As number of neutrons are 31.7% more than the protons.)
Hence, from or Eq. (i) 
x+1.317 x = 81
or 2.317 x = 81
or  {tex}x=\frac{81}{2.317}=34.958=35{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: An ion with mass number 37 possesses one unit of negative charge. If the ion contains 11.1% more neutrons than the electrons, find the symbol of the ion.
question_html: An ion with mass number 37 possesses one unit of negative charge. If the ion contains 11.1% more neutrons than the electrons, find the symbol of the ion.
solution_text: Let the no. of electron in the ion = x {tex}\therefore{/tex} the no. of protons = x - 1 (as the ion has one unit negative charge) and the no. of neutrons {tex}=\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 11.1}{100}{/tex} = 1.111 x Mass no. or mass of the ion = No. of protons + No. of neutrons {tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 1 + 1.111x = 37 or 2.111 x = 37 + 1 = 38 {tex}x=\frac{38}{2.111}=18{/tex} No. of electrons = 18 No. of protons = 18 - 1 = 17 Atomic no. of the ion = 17 Atom corresponding to ion = Cl Symbol of the ion = {tex}{}_{17}^{37}C{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the no. of electron in the ion = x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the no. of protons = x - 1 (as the ion has one unit negative charge)
and the no. of neutrons {tex}=\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 11.1}{100}{/tex}= 1.111 x
Mass no. or mass of the ion = No. of protons + No. of neutrons
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 1 + 1.111x = 37 or 2.111 x = 37 + 1 = 38
{tex}x=\frac{38}{2.111}=18{/tex}
No. of electrons = 18 No. of protons = 18 - 1 = 17
Atomic no. of the ion = 17 Atom corresponding to ion = Cl
Symbol of the ion = {tex}{}_{17}^{37}C{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_text: An ion with mass number 56 contains 3 units of positive charge and 30.4% more neutrons than the electrons. Assign the symbol to this ion.
question_html: An ion with mass number 56 contains 3 units of positive charge and 30.4% more neutrons than the electrons. Assign the symbol to this ion.
solution_text: Let the number of electrons in an ion A 3+ = x {tex}\therefore {/tex} Number or neutrons = x {tex}+\frac{30.4}{100} x{/tex} =1.304x (Number of neutrons are 30.4% more than the number of electrons). Number of electrons in neutral atom = x + 3 ( Since, The ion carries + 3 charge) So, number of protons in neutral atom = x + 3 Given, Mass number of ion = 56. We know that, mass number = n + p = 1.304x + x + 3 = 56 or 2.304x +3 = 56 or 2.304 x = 56 - 3 = 53. or {tex}x=\frac{53}{2.304}=23.003 \approx 23{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Number of protons = 23 + 3 = 26 = Atomic number Therefore, the symbol of the ion is {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe^{3+}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the number of electrons in an ion A3+= x
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Number or neutrons = x {tex}+\frac{30.4}{100} x{/tex} =1.304x
(Number of neutrons are 30.4% more than the number of electrons).
Number of electrons in neutral atom = x + 3 ( Since, The ion carries + 3 charge)
So, number of protons in neutral atom = x + 3

Given, Mass number of ion = 56.
We know that, mass number = n + p = 1.304x + x + 3 = 56
or 2.304x +3 = 56

or 2.304 x = 56 - 3 = 53.

 or {tex}x=\frac{53}{2.304}=23.003 \approx 23{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Number of protons = 23 + 3 = 26 = Atomic number
Therefore, the symbol of the ion is {tex}{}_{26}^{56}Fe^{3+}{/tex}.

question_text: Nitrogen laser produces a radiation at a wavelength of 337.1 nm. If the number of photons emitted is 5.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 24 , calculate the power of this laser.
question_html: Nitrogen laser produces a radiation at a wavelength of 337.1 nm. If the number of photons emitted is 5.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024, calculate the power of this laser.
solution_text: Power of the laser (E) = nhv = nh c/ {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(5.6 \times 10^{24}\right) \times\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(337.1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 J

question_html: In astronomical observations, signals observed from the distant starts are generally weak. If the photon detector receives a total of = 3.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J from the radiations of 600 nm. Calculate the number of photons received by the detector.
solution_text: Total energy received = 3.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m The energy of one photon, E = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 3.3125 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of photons = {tex}\frac{3.15 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{J}}{3.3125 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{/tex} = 10

The energy of one photon, E = {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex}

question_html: The longest wavelength doublet absorption transition is observed at 589 and 589.6 nm. Calculate the frequency of each transition and energy difference between two states.
solution_text: {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 589nm = {tex}589 \times 10^{-9}{/tex} v 1 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{589 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3000}{589} \times 10^{14} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} = 5.0934 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s -1 {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = 589.6 nm = 589.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m v 2 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{589.6 \times 10^{-9} m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3000}{589.6} \times 10^{14} s^{-1}{/tex} = 5.0882 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} E = E 1 - E 2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} = hc {tex}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right){/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{589 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}-\frac{1}{589.6 \times 10^{-9}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{19.878 \times 10^{-34} \times 10^{8}}{10^{-9}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{589.6-589}{589.6 \times 589}\right]{/tex} J {tex}\frac{19.878 \times 10^{-17} \times 0.6}{589.6 \times 589} \mathrm{J}{/tex} = 3.43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10- 22 J
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 589nm = {tex}589 \times 10^{-9}{/tex}
v1 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}}{589 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3000}{589} \times 10^{14} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} = 5.0934 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 s-1
{tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = 589.6 nm = 589.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m
v2 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{589.6 \times 10^{-9} m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3000}{589.6} \times 10^{14} s^{-1}{/tex} = 5.0882 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 s-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = E1 - E2 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}{/tex} = hc{tex}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right){/tex}{tex}\left[\frac{1}{589 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}-\frac{1}{589.6 \times 10^{-9}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{19.878 \times 10^{-34} \times 10^{8}}{10^{-9}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{589.6-589}{589.6 \times 589}\right]{/tex}J
{tex}\frac{19.878 \times 10^{-17} \times 0.6}{589.6 \times 589} \mathrm{J}{/tex} = 3.43 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 J

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The work function for caesium atom is 1.9 eV. Calculate the threshold wavelength and the threshold frequency of the radiation. If the caesium element is irradiated with a wavelength 500 nm, calculate the kinetic energy and the velocity of the ejected photoelectron.
question_html:
The work function for caesium atom is 1.9 eV. Calculate

  1. the threshold wavelength and
  2. the threshold frequency of the radiation. If the caesium element is irradiated with a wavelength 500 nm, calculate the kinetic energy and the velocity of the ejected photoelectron.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{E}_{0}=1.9 \mathrm{eV}=1.9 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} Threshold frequency (v 0 ) = {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.9 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex} = 459 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 s -1 = 4.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Threshold wavelength {tex}\left(\lambda_{0}\right)=\frac{c}{v_{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{4.59 \times 10^{14} s^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.6536 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 = 653.6 nm {tex}\approx{/tex} 654 nm {tex}E=E_{0}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{v}^{2}{/tex} Kinetic energy = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right)=E-E_{0}=h c\left[\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{500}-\frac{1}{654}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3 \times 154}{500 \times 654} \times 10^{-34+8+9}{/tex} = 9.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -20 J Velocity v = {tex}(v)=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.36 \times 10^{-20}}{m}} J{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.36 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2.057 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{20.57 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{m}^{2} s^{-2}}{/tex} = 4.5356 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 m/s
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{0}=1.9 \mathrm{eV}=1.9 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
Threshold frequency (v0) = {tex}\frac{E_{0}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.9 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex} = 459 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 s-1
= 4.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Threshold wavelength  {tex}\left(\lambda_{0}\right)=\frac{c}{v_{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{4.59 \times 10^{14} s^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.6536 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
= 653.6 nm {tex}\approx{/tex} 654 nm
{tex}E=E_{0}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{v}^{2}{/tex}
Kinetic energy =  {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right)=E-E_{0}=h c\left[\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{10^{-9} \mathrm{m}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{500}-\frac{1}{654}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3 \times 154}{500 \times 654} \times 10^{-34+8+9}{/tex} = 9.36 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20J
Velocity v = {tex}(v)=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.36 \times 10^{-20}}{m}} J{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.36 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{2.057 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{s}^{-2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{20.57 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{m}^{2} s^{-2}}{/tex}
 = 4.5356 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 m/s

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Following results are observed when sodium metal is irradiated with different wavelengths. Calculate threshold wavelength and Planck’s constant. {tex}\lambda{/tex} (nm) 500 450 400 v {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 (cm s -1 ) 2.55 4.35 5.35
question_html:
Following results are observed when sodium metal is irradiated with different wavelengths. Calculate

  1. threshold wavelength and
  2. Planck’s constant.
{tex}\lambda{/tex} (nm) 500 450 400
v {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 (cm s-1) 2.55 4.35 5.35
solution_text: Let us suppose threshold wavelength to be {tex}\lambda{/tex} nm the kinetic energy of the radiation is given as: h (v-v o ) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} mv 2 or {tex}h c\left(1 / \lambda-1 / \lambda_{0}\right)=1 / 2 \mathrm{mv}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{hc}\left[\frac{1}{500 \times 10^{9}}-\frac{1}{\lambda \times 10^{-9}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[2.55 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-2}\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{10^{-9}}\right]\left[\frac{1}{500}-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[2.55 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}{/tex} ...[i] Similarly, we can also write, {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{10^{-9}}\right]\left[\frac{1}{450}-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[4.35 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}{/tex} ...[ii] {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{10^{-9}}\right]\left[\frac{1}{400}-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[5.35 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}{/tex} ...[iii] Dividing equation [iii] and [i] {tex}\frac{\left[\frac{\lambda-400}{400 \lambda}\right]}{\left[\frac{\lambda-500}{500 \lambda}\right]}=\frac{\left[5.35 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[2.55 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{5 \lambda-2000}{4 \lambda-2000}=\frac{[5.35]^{2}}{[2.55]^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{5 \lambda-2000}{4 \lambda-2000}{/tex} = 4.40177 17.6070 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 5 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 8803.537 - 2000 {tex}\lambda=[6805.537] /[12.607]{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 539.8 nm The wavelength is 540 nm.
solution_html:
Let us suppose threshold wavelength to be {tex}\lambda{/tex} nm the kinetic energy of the radiation is given as:
h (v-vo) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} mv2
or
{tex}h c\left(1 / \lambda-1 / \lambda_{0}\right)=1 / 2 \mathrm{mv}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{hc}\left[\frac{1}{500 \times 10^{9}}-\frac{1}{\lambda \times 10^{-9}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[2.55 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-2}\right]^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{10^{-9}}\right]\left[\frac{1}{500}-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[2.55 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}{/tex} ...[i]
Similarly, we can also write,
{tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{10^{-9}}\right]\left[\frac{1}{450}-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[4.35 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}{/tex} ...[ii]
{tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{10^{-9}}\right]\left[\frac{1}{400}-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{m}\left[5.35 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}{/tex} ...[iii]
Dividing equation [iii] and [i]
{tex}\frac{\left[\frac{\lambda-400}{400 \lambda}\right]}{\left[\frac{\lambda-500}{500 \lambda}\right]}=\frac{\left[5.35 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[2.55 \times 10^{3}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{5 \lambda-2000}{4 \lambda-2000}=\frac{[5.35]^{2}}{[2.55]^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{5 \lambda-2000}{4 \lambda-2000}{/tex} = 4.40177
17.6070{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 5{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 8803.537 - 2000
{tex}\lambda=[6805.537] /[12.607]{/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 539.8 nm
The wavelength is 540 nm.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: The ejection of the photoelectrons from the silver metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying the voltage of 0.35 V when the radiation 256.7 nm is used. Calculate the work function for silver metal.
question_html: The ejection of the photoelectrons from the silver metal in the photoelectric effect experiment can be stopped by applying the voltage of 0.35 V when the radiation 256.7 nm is used. Calculate the work function for silver metal.
solution_text: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 256.7 nm = 256.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = K.E. = 0.35 eV E= {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(256.7 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3}{256.7} \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{J}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3 \times 10^{-17}}{256.7 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{662.6 \times 3}{256.7 \times 1.602} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 4.83 eV = E = E o + K.E. 4.83eV = E o + 0.35eV E o = 4.83 - 0.35 = 4.48 eV = 4.48 eV
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 256.7 nm = 256.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m
= K.E. = 0.35 eV
E= {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(256.7 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3}{256.7} \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{J}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3 \times 10^{-17}}{256.7 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{662.6 \times 3}{256.7 \times 1.602} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}  = 4.83 eV
= E = Eo + K.E.
4.83eV = Eo + 0.35eV
Eo = 4.83 - 0.35 = 4.48 eV = 4.48 eV

subject: Chemistry
question_text: If the photon of the wavelength 150 pm strikes an atom, and one of its inner bound electrons is ejected out with a velocity of 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ms -1 . Calculate the energy with which is a bound to the nucleus.
question_html: If the photon of the wavelength 150 pm strikes an atom, and one of its inner bound electrons is ejected out with a velocity of 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex}107 ms-1. Calculate the energy with which is a bound to the nucleus.
solution_text: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 150pm = 150 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 m = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m; v = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ma -1 K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg {tex}\times{/tex} (1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 ms -1 ) 2 = {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 1.5 \times 1.5}{2} \times{/tex} 10 -31+14 J = 10.2375 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J = 1.02375 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J E = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3}{1.5} \times{/tex} 10 -34+8+10 = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3}{1.5} \times{/tex} 10 -16 J = 13.386 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J E = E 0 + K.E E 0 = E - k.E = (13.386 - 1.024) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J = 12.362 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J = {tex}\frac{12.362 \times 10^{-16}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 7.7167 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 eV
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 150pm = 150 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 m = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m; v = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 ma-1
K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg {tex}\times{/tex}(1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 ms-1)2
= {tex}\frac{9.1 \times 1.5 \times 1.5}{2} \times{/tex}10-31+14 J
= 10.2375 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J = 1.02375 {tex}\times{/tex}10-16 J
E = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3}{1.5} \times{/tex} 10-34+8+10
= {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 3}{1.5} \times{/tex} 10-16 J = 13.386 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 J
E = E0 + K.E
E0 = E - k.E = (13.386 - 1.024) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 J
= 12.362 {tex}\times{/tex}10-16 J = {tex}\frac{12.362 \times 10^{-16}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 7.7167 {tex}\times{/tex}103 eV

question_text: Emission transitions in the Paschen series end at orbit n = 3 and start from orbit n and can be represeted as v = 3.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 (Hz) [1/3 2 - 1/n 2 ] Calculate the value of n if the transition is observed at 1285 nm. Find the region of the spectrum.
question_html: Emission transitions in the Paschen series end at orbit n = 3 and start from orbit n and can be represeted as v = 3.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 (Hz) [1/32 - 1/n2] Calculate the value of n if the transition is observed at 1285 nm. Find the region of the spectrum.
solution_text: v = (3.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1285 nm = 1285 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 1.285 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(1.285 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 2.3346 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 s -1 2.3346 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 = 3.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 {tex}\left[\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{2.3346}{32.9}=\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{/tex} or 0.71 = {tex}\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{n^{2}}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex} - 0.071 = 0.111 - 0.071 = 0.04 n 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{0.04}{/tex} = 25 or n = 5 Paschen series lies in infrared region of the spectrum.
solution_html:
v = (3.29 {tex}\times{/tex}1015 Hz) {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1285 nm = 1285 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m = 1.285 {tex}\times{/tex}10-6 m
v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)}{\left(1.285 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m}\right)}{/tex} = 2.3346 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014s-1
2.3346 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 = 3.29 {tex}\times{/tex}1015 {tex}\left[\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2.3346}{32.9}=\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{/tex}or 0.71 = {tex}\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{n^{2}}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}- 0.071 = 0.111 - 0.071 = 0.04
n2 = {tex}\frac{1}{0.04}{/tex}= 25 or n = 5

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Calculate the wavelength for the emission transition if it starts from the orbit having radius 1.3225 nm and ends at 211.6 pm. Name the series to which this transition belongs and the region of the spectrum.
question_html: Calculate the wavelength for the emission transition if it starts from the orbit having radius 1.3225 nm and ends at 211.6 pm. Name the series to which this transition belongs and the region of the spectrum.
solution_text: The radius of the n the orbit of hydrogen-like particles is given by the expression, {tex}r = \frac{{0.529{n^2}}}{Z}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} {tex}r=\frac{5.29 n^{2}}{Z} p m{/tex} For radius (r1) = 1.3225 nm =1322.5pm {tex}=1.32225 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}=1322.25 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}=1322.25 p m \quad n_{1}^{2}=\frac{r_{1} Z}{52.9}{/tex} {tex}n_{1}^{2}=\frac{1322.25 Z}{52.9}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}n_{2}^{2}=\frac{211.6 Z}{52.9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_{1}^{2}}{n_{2}^{2}}=\frac{1322.5}{211.6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_{1}^{2}}{n_{2}^{2}}=6.25{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=2.5{/tex} {tex}\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{25}{10}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n 1 = 5 and n 2 = 2 Therefore, the electron transition is from the 5th orbit to the 2nd orbit and it, therefore, corresponds to the Balmer series. The wavenumber {tex}(\bar{\nu}){/tex} of the transition is: {tex}1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{5^{2}}\right) m^{-1}{/tex} {tex}=1.097 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}\left(\frac{21}{100}\right){/tex} {tex}=2.303 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m}^{-1}{/tex} Wavelength ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) of the emitted radiation is: {tex}\lambda=\frac{1}{\nu}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2.303 \times 10^{6} m^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}=0.434 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m} \lambda{/tex} = 434 nm The transition lies in the visible region.
solution_html:
The radius of the n the orbit of hydrogen-like particles is given by the expression,
{tex}r = \frac{{0.529{n^2}}}{Z}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}
{tex}r=\frac{5.29 n^{2}}{Z} p m{/tex}
For radius (r1) = 1.3225 nm =1322.5pm
{tex}=1.32225 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}=1322.25 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}=1322.25 p m \quad n_{1}^{2}=\frac{r_{1} Z}{52.9}{/tex}
{tex}n_{1}^{2}=\frac{1322.25 Z}{52.9}{/tex}
Similarly,
{tex}n_{2}^{2}=\frac{211.6 Z}{52.9}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n_{1}^{2}}{n_{2}^{2}}=\frac{1322.5}{211.6}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n_{1}^{2}}{n_{2}^{2}}=6.25{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=2.5{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{25}{10}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n1 = 5 and n2 = 2
Therefore, the electron transition is from the 5th orbit to the 2nd orbit and it, therefore, corresponds to the Balmer series. The wavenumber {tex}(\bar{\nu}){/tex} of the transition is:
{tex}1.097 \times 10^{7}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{5^{2}}\right) m^{-1}{/tex}
{tex}=1.097 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{m}^{-1}\left(\frac{21}{100}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2.303 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m}^{-1}{/tex}
Wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) of the emitted radiation is:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{1}{\nu}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2.303 \times 10^{6} m^{-1}}{/tex}
{tex}=0.434 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{m} \lambda{/tex}
= 434 nm The transition lies in the visible region.

question_text: If the position of the electron is measured within an accuracy of {tex}\pm{/tex} 0.002 nm, calculate the uncertainty in the momentum of the electron. Suppose the momentum of the electron is h/4 {tex}\pi_m{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 nm, is there any problem in defining this value.
question_html: If the position of the electron is measured within an accuracy of {tex}\pm{/tex}0.002 nm, calculate the uncertainty in the momentum of the electron. Suppose the momentum of the electron is h/4{tex}\pi_m{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 nm, is there any problem in defining this value.
solution_text: As per Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle, {tex}\triangle x.\triangle p > = \frac {4}{4 \pi}{/tex} Where, {tex}\triangle{/tex} x = uncertainty in the position of the electron {tex}\triangle{/tex} p = uncertainty in the momentum of the electron Substituting the given values in the expression for Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle : = 2.637 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -23 J sm -1 {tex}\triangle{/tex} p = 2.637 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -23 kg.m.s -1 (1J = 1 kg ms 2 s -1 ) Uncertainty in the momentum of the electron = 2.637 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–23 kg.ms -1 = 1.055 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 kg.ms -1 . This is larger than actual momentum. The value cannot be defined because the magnitude of the actual momentum is much smaller than the uncertainty.
solution_html:
As per Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle, {tex}\triangle x.\triangle p > = \frac {4}{4 \pi}{/tex}
Where,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}x = uncertainty in the position of the electron
{tex}\triangle{/tex}p = uncertainty in the momentum of the electron
Substituting the given values in the expression for Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle :
= 2.637 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 J sm-1
{tex}\triangle{/tex}p = 2.637 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-23 kg.m.s-1 (1J = 1 kg ms2s-1)
Uncertainty in the momentum of the electron = 2.637 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–23 kg.ms-1 = 1.055 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 kg.ms-1. This is larger than actual momentum.
The value cannot be defined because the magnitude of the actual momentum is much smaller than the uncertainty.

The quantum numbers of six electrons are given below. Arrange them in order of increasing energy. If any of these combination(s) has/have the same energy lists:

solution_text: Electrons i, ii, iii, iv, v, and vi reside in the 4d, 3d, 4p, 3d, 3p, and 4p orbitals (respectively). Therefore increasing order of energies: v (3p) < ii(3d)=iv(3d) < iii(4p) = vi(4p) < i(4d).

question_text: The bromine atom possesses 35 electrons. It contains 6 electrons in 2p orbital, 6 electron in 3p orbital and 5 electrons in 4p orbital. Which of these electrons experience low effective nuclear charge?
question_html: The bromine atom possesses 35 electrons. It contains 6 electrons in 2p orbital, 6 electron in 3p orbital and 5 electrons in 4p orbital. Which of these electrons experience low effective nuclear charge?
solution_text: 4p electron experiences lowest effective nuclear charge because of the maximum magnitude screening or shielding effect. It is farthest from the nucleus.
solution_html: 4p electron experiences lowest effective nuclear charge because of the maximum magnitude screening or shielding effect. It is farthest from the nucleus.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Among the following pairs of orbitals which orbital will experience the larger effective nuclear charge? 2s and 3s 4d and 4f 3p and 3f.
question_html:
Among the following pairs of orbitals which orbital will experience the larger effective nuclear charge?

  1. 2s and 3s
  2. 4d and 4f
  3. 3p and 3f.
solution_text: Nuclear charge is defined as the net positive charge experienced by an electron in the orbital of a multi-electron atom. The closer the orbital, the greater is the nuclear charge experienced by the electron (s) in it. 2s is closer to the nucleus than 3s.Hence 2s will experience larger effective nuclear charge. 4d will experience greater nuclear charge than 4f since 4d is closer to the nucleus than 4f. 3p will experience greater nuclear charge since it is closer to the nucleus than 3f because 3p is closer to nucleus than 3f.
solution_html:
Nuclear charge is defined as the net positive charge experienced by an electron in the orbital of a multi-electron atom. The closer the orbital, the greater is the nuclear charge experienced by the electron (s) in it.

  1. 2s is closer to the nucleus than 3s.Hence 2s will experience larger effective nuclear charge.
  2. 4d will experience greater nuclear charge than 4f since 4d is closer to the nucleus than 4f.
  3. 3p will experience greater nuclear charge since it is closer to the nucleus than 3f because 3p is closer to nucleus than 3f.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: How many subshells are associated with n = 4? How many electrons will be present in the subshell having m s value of - 1 /2 for n=4?
question_html:
1. How many subshells are associated with n = 4?
  2. How many electrons will be present in the subshell having ms value of - 1 /2 for n=4?
solution_text: (i) For, Principal quantum number, n = 4, Azimuthal quantum number, l = 0,1, 2 and 3, i.e., s p d and f (4 subshells). (ii) Number of orbitals in 4th shell = n 2 = 4 2 = 16 The maximum number of electrons present in any orbital is two (2) with opposite spin and each orbital has one electrons with m s = -1/2. Hence, there are 16 electrons with m s = -1/2.
solution_html:
(i) For, Principal quantum number, n = 4, Azimuthal quantum number, l = 0,1, 2 and 3, i.e., s p d and f (4 subshells).
(ii) Number of orbitals in 4th shell = n2 = 42 = 16
The maximum number of electrons present in any orbital is two (2) with opposite spin and each orbital has one electrons with ms = -1/2. Hence, there are 16 electrons with ms= -1/2.
